title: Issuance as a market-top signal — equity and debt (incl. the NVIDIA $25bn bond test)
question: Does a wave of issuance — equity (IPOs + secondaries) or debt (the AI-era bond boom, e.g. NVIDIA's June-2026 $25bn deal) — mark an overpriced market and predict weak forward returns?
finding: No usable timing signal for either. Equity (Part I): across 2001–2026 (Nasdaq Composite + SPY/QQQ/RSP) with survivorship-free EDGAR filing counts, 0 of 12 non-overlapping annual tests are significant; a faint IPO/SPAC froth tilt is ~half momentum and 2020–22-bound; the "SEOs invert" result is an EDGAR coverage-ratio artifact. Debt (Part II): same null — 0 of 4 primary annual tests, the in-sample tilt is crisis-bound and collapses out-of-sample, the one significant secondary cell is noise, and the Baker–Wurgler equity/debt share puts a debt-tilted mix on the historically benign side. NVIDIA's $25bn debt-over-equity choice is not an equity-top signal; the legitimate worry it belongs to is credit complacency (IG OAS ~73bp / HY ~266bp), a channel this issuance-timing study does not test. The robust effect is cross-sectional (study 15), not market timing.
